title: Polyurethane Transfer on Polyester — Dual-Solvent Extraction
stainChemistry: Polyurethane coating transfer typically occurs from branded bags (canvas bags with polyurethane coating), packaging, or garment bags where the coating has degraded. The polyurethane polymer (a thermosetting elastomer) transfers to polyester through direct contact and heat. The polyurethane bonds to polyester through both mechanical adhesion (filling surface voids) and weak van der Waals interactions between the polymer chains. The transfer appears as a tan, brown, or clear waxy stain that is extremely resistant to water-based spotting.
whyThisWorks: Polyurethane is a hydrocarbon-based polymer that dissolves in organic solvents, particularly volatile dry-clean solvents (VDS like perchloroethylene or Stoddard solvent) and amyl acetate. The treatment uses a two-solvent approach: first VDS to initially soften and lift the polyurethane coating, then amyl acetate to dissolve and extract the remaining polymer. This dual-solvent approach is more effective than either solvent alone. The key is dissolving the polymer rather than trying to bleach it (which won't work on synthetics).

spottingProtocol.agent: volatile dry-clean solvent (VDS: perchloroethylene or Stoddard solvent)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, glass or stainless steel container for solvent, gentle pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge VDS into the polyurethane stain from the back side. Work carefully — use enough solvent to saturate the stain but avoid over-saturating the polyester (excess solvent can dissolve polyester dyes). The stain will gradually soften and lighten. Replace the under-pad as it absorbs dissolved polymer. This step lifts the bulk of the polyurethane.

spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, glass or stainless container, gentle pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge amyl acetate into the remaining polyurethane stain. Amyl acetate is a stronger solvent for polyurethane than VDS and will dissolve any remaining polymer residue. Work from outside the stain inward. Replace the under-pad frequently as it absorbs dissolved polymer. The stain should fade significantly during this step.

spottingProtocol.agent: amyl acetate or petroleum ether (final extraction)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final light sponge with amyl acetate or petroleum ether to remove any remaining polymer residue and rinse out the dissolved polyurethane. This completes the dissolution and extraction of the stain. Work gently to avoid spreading residual polymer.

spottingProtocol.technique: air dry on spotting board with ventilation
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting board, ventilation fan
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place the garment on a spotting board and allow to air dry completely in a well-ventilated area. All solvent residue must evaporate. Do NOT force dry with heat — this can set any remaining polymer residue. Gentle air circulation is best.

escalation: If polyurethane stain remains after the full protocol, the polymer may have fully cured or cross-linked with the polyester surface, making it insoluble in standard solvents. Professional dry-clean treatment with stronger solvents or with the addition of detergent to penetrate polyurethane is the only remaining option. However, if the polyurethane is fully cured, it may be permanent. Mechanical methods (gentle scraping) should NEVER be attempted — they damage polyester fibers.
commonMistakes: Using water-based spotting — polyurethane is hydrophobic and will not dissolve in water or aqueous surfactants.
Using heat — polyester is thermoplastic and heat accelerates polyurethane bonding.
Scrubbing or rubbing the stain — this embeds the polymer deeper and damages polyester fibers.
Using amyl acetate first without VDS — sometimes a two-solvent approach is necessary for thick polymer coatings.
scienceNote: The two-solvent approach reflects understanding polymer chemistry. Volatile dry-clean solvents (like perchloroethylene) are good general solvents for many polymers and lipids but are not optimized for polyurethane. Amyl acetate is a stronger, more selective solvent for polyurethane because of its ester functionality, which has higher polarity than VDS. Sequential use of a weaker solvent (VDS) followed by a stronger solvent (amyl acetate) is more efficient than using amyl acetate alone, which could over-saturate the polyester and damage dyes.
safety: CRITICAL: NEVER use amyl acetate on acetate or triacetate fibers — it will dissolve the fiber completely. Test first on any fabric with uncertain fiber content. Both VDS and amyl acetate require excellent ventilation — use in fume hoods or well-ventilated areas. Wear nitrile gloves (not latex — solvents penetrate latex). Avoid skin contact and inhalation. Do not mix solvents. Follow local regulations for solvent disposal.
materialWarnings: Polyester is resistant to these solvents but polyester dyes can be affected by strong organic solvents.
Always test on an interior seam or hidden area first.
Some polyester blends may have different solvent resistance — check fiber content.
NEVER use amyl acetate on acetate, triacetate, or any cellulose acetate fibers.
Do not use heat after solvent treatment — polyester is thermoplastic and heat can set residual polymer.
callPro: For valuable polyester garments or complex multi-fiber blends, professional dry-clean treatment is safer. Professional dry cleaners have access to stronger solvents and controlled environments. For garments with unknown fiber content or if you're unsure about fiber stability with organic solvents, professional treatment is recommended.
customerExplanation: Polyurethane coating transfers from bags or packaging through direct contact. Unlike water-soluble stains, polyurethane is a polymer that requires organic solvents to dissolve. We used volatile dry-clean solvent first to soften and lift the coating, then amyl acetate to dissolve and extract any remaining polymer. The garment was allowed to air dry completely to evaporate all solvent residue.
homeSolutions: Rubbing alcohol (70% isopropyl alcohol) sponged into the stain — weaker than professional solvents but may lighten fresh polyurethane stains. Test first on a seam.

Mineral spirits or paint thinner sponged gently into the stain — these are weak aromatic hydrocarbons that may partially dissolve fresh polyurethane, but are less effective than amyl acetate. Use in ventilated areas only.

For very light polymer stains, sometimes the transfer is thin enough that mechanical removal (gentle rubbing with a plastic scraper or soft brush) combined with heat can shrink the polymer slightly, but this is risky on polyester.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Amyl Acetate (n-Amyl Acetate / Banana Oil)
products.amazonSearchQuery: amyl acetate n-amyl acetate cleaning solvent
products.note: Essential for polyurethane dissolution. Stronger solvent for polyurethane than VDS alone. Requires excellent ventilation and nitrile gloves. NEVER use on acetate fibers.

aftercare: Allow the treated area to air dry. The VDS will evaporate, leaving the polyurethane partially dissolved and lifted. This is a transition step between solvents. Ensure good ventilation — VDS vapors are strong.
